preamble: As part of Grimm's Law, Proto-Indo-European voiceless plosives became voiceless fricatives in Proto-Germanic.

examples.from: Bilabial stop [p] in certain phonological contexts
examples.to: [f] or labial fricative in contact varieties
examples.note: Georgian maintains a three-way laryngeal contrast (voiced/voiceless/ejective) in stops, but bilabial fricativization can occur in modern urban varieties under contact with Russian.
Historical fricativization of initial *p. E.g., Proto-Vietic *pa → Vietnamese 'ba' [ɓa] (three) - wait, ba is b. 'phi' [fi] (to fly) from Middle Chinese or similar.
Free variation: /p/ and /f/ are often in free variation or act as allophones for many speakers (e.g., 'sapos' ~ 'safos' meaning 'if').
Yiddish deaffricated MHG initial /pf/ → /f/ word-initially. E.g., MHG pfeffer → Yiddish fefer (pepper), MHG pflanze → Yiddish flants (plant). Medially/finally /pf/ → /p/.

Beged-Kefet spirantization: pe (פ) is realized as [p] word-initially or after a consonant, and as [f] (or bilabial [ɸ]) after a vowel. Marked by dagesh: פּ [p] vs. פ [f]. The same root appears in Arabic as فَارِس fāris with /f/ in all positions, showing Arabic generalized the fricative while Hebrew preserves the allophonic alternation. Tiberian Hebrew pronunciation (c. 7th–10th century CE).
Proto-Semitic *p → Arabic /f/ in all positions; Arabic has no /p/ phoneme. Cf. also Hebrew פָּרַשׁ pārash vs. Arabic فَارِس fāris (horseman).
Latin pater, Greek patēr retain the [p].

Partial Grimm's Law in Iranian: voiceless stop /p/ became fricative /f/ before another consonant. PIE *sapta 'seven' → Proto-Iranian *hafta (cf. Persian هفت haft, Avestan hapta). Compare Sanskrit sapta, which preserves *p.
The PIE aspirated *ph → Iranian /f/ in many cluster positions. Modern Persian panj 'five' < Proto-Iranian *panca, showing partial preservation of /p/ word-initially without a following consonant.
Proto-Iranian *pr > *fr spirantization is reflected in Kurdish farwâr 'present' (< *pra-bara- 'brought forward'). The p-to-f shift in consonant clusters is a hallmark of the Iranian branch.

sources: Windfuhr, G. (2009). The Iranian Languages. Routledge.
